title: Asking For and Giving Advice
description: Learn how to ask for advice politely and give useful advice using should, shouldn't, ought to, and why don't you / you could.

content.explanation:
We use SHOULD to give advice or recommendations. We use SHOULDN'T to advise someone NOT to do something.

✅ SHOULD + base verb:
• You should drink more water.
• She should see a doctor.
• They should leave early to avoid traffic.

❌ SHOULDN'T + base verb:
• You shouldn't eat too much junk food.
• He shouldn't skip his classes.
• You shouldn't stay up so late.

🔑 KEY PHRASES FOR ADVICE:
• I think you should... → I think you should talk to your manager.
• You really should... → You really should try this restaurant!
• If I were you, I would... → If I were you, I would apologise.
• My advice would be to... → My advice would be to practise every day.
• Why don't you...? → Why don't you take a day off?
examples: You should apply for that job — you're well qualified.
She shouldn't drink coffee so late at night.
If I were you, I would speak to the teacher directly.
Why don't you try a different approach?
content.tip: SHOULD is a suggestion or recommendation — it is NOT as strong as MUST or HAVE TO.

content.explanation:
There are several useful ways to ASK for advice in English:

❓ Common expressions:
• What should I do?
• What do you think I should do?
• What would you do if you were me?
• Do you think I should...?
• Any advice on...?
• What's your suggestion?
• Should I...?
• Do you have any tips for...?

📌 OUGHT TO — another way to give advice:
• You ought to see a doctor. (= You should see a doctor.)
• He ought to apologise. (= He should apologise.)
• OUGHT TO is slightly more formal but means the same as SHOULD.

📌 COULD — softer advice:
• You could try calling them again.
• She could take a break if she's tired.
• COULD is gentler — it gives options without pressure.
examples: What do you think I should do about this problem?
You ought to register early — places fill up fast.
You could speak to the HR department about this.
If I were you, I'd take the job offer.
content.tip: OUGHT TO = SHOULD (same meaning). COULD is softer — it suggests options, not obligations.

vocabulary.meaning: to suggest something as good or suitable
vocabulary.example: I recommend this restaurant — the food is excellent.

vocabulary.meaning: to mention an idea for someone to consider
vocabulary.example: I suggest you arrive early to avoid the rush.
You should consider all your options before deciding.
Don't hesitate to call me if you need help.

vocabulary.meaning: to seek advice or information from an expert
vocabulary.example: You should consult a lawyer before signing anything.

vocabulary.meaning: to think again about a decision
vocabulary.example: I think you should reconsider — it's a big decision.

vocabulary.meaning: sensible and useful in real situations
vocabulary.example: His advice was very practical and easy to follow.

vocabulary.meaning: a way of thinking about something
vocabulary.example: Try to see it from her perspective.

content.instruction: Listen and repeat these common advice expressions. Try to sound natural and friendly.

items.text: I think you should talk to your manager about it.
If I were you, I would take the job.
Why don't you try speaking to her directly?
You really shouldn't skip breakfast every day.
What do you think I should do about this?

tasks.question: Ravi has a headache and hasn't slept well. What is the BEST advice?
options: You should work harder tonight.
You shouldn't rest — you have a lot to do.
You should rest and drink plenty of water.
You ought to eating more food.
tasks.correct_answer: You should rest and drink plenty of water.
tasks.explanation: Rest and hydration are the most sensible advice for a headache and poor sleep.

tasks.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
options: You ought to seeing a doctor.
You ought to see a doctor.
You ought see a doctor.
You ought to saw a doctor.
tasks.correct_answer: You ought to see a doctor.
tasks.explanation: OUGHT TO is followed by the BASE VERB. Unlike 'must' and 'should', 'ought to' keeps the 'to'.

tasks.question: Kavya wants to improve her English. What is a SOFT piece of advice using COULD?
options: You must watch English films.
You should watch English films every day.
You could watch English films — it really helps.
You mustn't miss any English classes.
tasks.correct_answer: You could watch English films — it really helps.
tasks.explanation: COULD gives a gentler suggestion — it presents an option rather than a strong obligation.

tasks.question: Your friend says 'I have a big job interview tomorrow but I'm very nervous. What should I do?' Which response asks for clarification before advising?
options: You should just relax.
What kind of job is it? Tell me more and I can give better advice.
You mustn't be nervous.
You ought to cancel the interview.
tasks.correct_answer: What kind of job is it? Tell me more and I can give better advice.
tasks.explanation: Good advisors ask for more information before giving advice — this shows care and helps give relevant suggestions.

tasks.instruction: Fill in the blank with should, shouldn't, or ought to.

items.sentence: You _____ eat more fruits and vegetables. They are very good for your health.
You _____ stay up so late every night — you look exhausted.
items.hint: This is advice to stop a bad habit.

items.sentence: You _____ apply for that scholarship. You have excellent grades.
items.hint: This is a recommendation — similar to 'should'.

tasks.instruction: Complete the sentence with an appropriate advice phrase from the box: Why don't you / If I were you / You could / I think you should.

items.sentence: _____ talk to your professor directly about this issue.
_____, I would apologise to her right away.
items.hint: Giving advice from your own perspective.

items.sentence: _____ take a different route to avoid the traffic on MG Road?

tasks.instruction: Rearrange the words to make correct advice sentences.

items.answer: I think you should apply for the scholarship.
If I were you, I would talk to a doctor.

tasks.instruction: Rearrange the words to make correct advice sentences.

items.answer: You shouldn't eat so much fried food.
Why don't you try a different approach?

tasks.instruction: Match the problem with the best piece of advice.

pairs.left: I always feel tired in the morning.
pairs.right: You should go to bed earlier.
You could practise more before trying again.

pairs.left: My English pronunciation is not clear.
pairs.right: I think you should listen to more English podcasts.
If I were you, I would make a study schedule.

pairs.left: I haven't spoken to my friend in weeks.
pairs.right: Why don't you give her a call today?

dialogue.context: Sonal is feeling stressed about her job situation. She is talking to her friend Priya at a café in Mumbai.

turns.speaker: Sonal
turns.text: Priya, I really need some advice. I'm not happy at my current job, but I'm scared to leave.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: Tell me more. What exactly is making you unhappy?

turns.speaker: Sonal
turns.text: My manager is very difficult. He never appreciates my work and the salary is low. I've been there for two years.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: I think you should speak to your HR department first. Have you tried that?

turns.speaker: Sonal
turns.text: No, I haven't. I'm scared they won't listen.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: You ought to try — you have nothing to lose. If they don't respond, then you can think about leaving.

turns.speaker: Sonal
turns.text: That's true. But if I leave, what should I do? I don't have another job lined up.

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: If I were you, I would start applying while I'm still employed. It's much easier to find a job when you already have one.

turns.speaker: Sonal
turns.text: That makes sense. Do you think I should update my LinkedIn profile?

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: Absolutely! You should update it this week and connect with people in your industry.

turns.speaker: Sonal
turns.text: I'm also thinking of doing a certification course. Should I?

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: You could do that — it would definitely strengthen your CV. Why don't you check what's available online?

turns.speaker: Sonal
turns.text: You're right. I shouldn't just complain — I should take action!

turns.speaker: Priya
turns.text: Exactly! And don't worry — I'm here to help. You've got this!

comprehension_questions.question: What is Sonal's main problem at work?
options: She earns too much
Her manager is difficult and the salary is low
She has too many meetings
She works too far from home
comprehension_questions.correct_answer: Her manager is difficult and the salary is low

comprehension_questions.question: What does Priya recommend Sonal do FIRST?
options: Resign immediately
Apply for new jobs
Speak to the HR department
Do a certification course

comprehension_questions.question: What does Priya say about job searching?
options: You should quit first, then look
It's easier to find a job when you already have one
You shouldn't apply for new jobs
You ought to take a long holiday first
comprehension_questions.correct_answer: It's easier to find a job when you already have one

tasks.instruction: Find and correct the mistake in each sentence.

items.sentence: You ought see a doctor about that cough.
items.explanation: OUGHT TO always keeps 'to' before the base verb.

items.sentence: If I was you, I would ask for help.
items.explanation: 'If I were you' is the correct fixed phrase — always use 'were', not 'was'.

items.sentence: Why you don't take a break?
items.explanation: The correct question structure is 'Why don't you + base verb?'

tasks.scenario: Your colleague Rajesh tells you he has been having trouble sleeping for two weeks. He also has a headache every morning. He asks what he should do.
tasks.question: What is the most helpful and appropriate advice?
options: You mustn't sleep at all — it will make it worse.
I think you should see a doctor, and you could also try reducing screen time before bed.
You don't need to do anything — it will pass.
If I were you, I would drink more coffee to stay awake.
tasks.correct_answer: I think you should see a doctor, and you could also try reducing screen time before bed.
tasks.explanation: This combines a direct recommendation (should) and a gentle suggestion (could), which is well-balanced and practical advice.

tasks.instruction: Your friend wants to improve their English. Give them at least 5 pieces of advice using should, shouldn't, could, and why don't you. Speak for at least 60 seconds.
tasks.prompt: Give advice to a friend who wants to improve their English skills.
tasks.example_response: If you want to improve your English, I have some good advice for you. First, I think you should watch English movies and TV shows with English subtitles — not Hindi subtitles. This really helps you understand natural speech. You could also listen to English podcasts when you travel or during free time. Why don't you keep a small notebook and write down new words you learn every day? You should also try speaking English with friends or classmates — don't be shy! You shouldn't worry about making mistakes — everyone makes mistakes and that is how you learn. I also think you ought to read an English newspaper or news website for at least ten minutes every day. And you shouldn't translate word by word — try to think in English directly. If you follow all this advice, your English will improve very quickly!

tasks.instruction: Think of a problem you have or had recently — at work, at home, with studies, or with health. Describe the problem and ask for advice. Then give yourself the advice you think you need.
tasks.prompt: Describe a problem and ask for / give yourself advice.
tasks.example_response: I have a problem with time management. I always feel like I have too much to do and not enough time. I procrastinate a lot and leave things to the last minute. What should I do? Well, I think I should make a proper schedule every morning and list the things I need to do in order of priority. I ought to stop checking my phone every few minutes — it's a huge distraction. I could also use a timer to focus for 25 minutes without interruptions, and then take a 5-minute break. I shouldn't try to do everything at once — I should focus on one task at a time. If I were someone giving me advice, I would say: stop overthinking and just start! Even small progress is better than nothing.

tasks.instruction: Complete this advice paragraph using should, shouldn't, ought to, could, or if I were you.
tasks.paragraph: Meera wants to start exercising regularly. Here is some advice for her. She (1)_____ start with short workouts of 20-30 minutes rather than trying to run for an hour on day one. She (2)_____ exercise on an empty stomach — that can cause dizziness. (3)_____ she also join a local yoga or fitness class. She (4)_____ try different types of exercise to find what she enjoys. (5)_____, I would start walking for 30 minutes every morning — it's free and easy to do anywhere. She (6)_____ forget to drink enough water before and after exercising. Consistency is the most important thing — she (7)_____ give up after just a few days.
tasks.hint: Think about which word is the most natural in each situation.

what_you_learned: SHOULD + base verb → giving advice (You should rest.)
SHOULDN'T + base verb → advising against something (You shouldn't skip meals.)
OUGHT TO + base verb → same as should, slightly more formal
COULD → softer suggestion / option (You could try yoga.)
WHY DON'T YOU + base verb? → friendly advice question
IF I WERE YOU, I WOULD → advice from your perspective
Key phrases for asking: What should I do? / Do you think I should...?
Key vocab: recommend, suggest, consider, consult, practical, perspective
content.grammar_summary: Use SHOULD and OUGHT TO for recommendations. Use COULD and WHY DON'T YOU for softer suggestions. Always use 'If I WERE you' (not 'was').
content.encouragement: Fantastic! Giving and asking for advice is one of the most useful skills in real conversations. You now have many tools to do this naturally. Keep practising! 🌟
